race.name: Vasaloppet Japan
race.tagline: Forty-five kilometers of Hokkaido powder — Japan's tribute to the Swedish original, three laps through the snow country where Siberian winds meet volcanic terrain.

vitals.aid_stations: Feed stations with Japanese refreshments and energy food

climate.description: March in Asahikawa still delivers full winter conditions. Hokkaido is one of the snowiest inhabited regions on Earth, with cold Siberian air masses picking up moisture over the Sea of Japan. Typically -10°C to -2°C. Deep, reliable snow but sometimes heavy snowfall during the race.
challenges: Heavy snowfall possible during the race
Hokkaido's deep powder can affect grooming quality
Cold Siberian winds across open sections
Long travel distance for most international participants

course.primary: A 45km skate race on a 15km loop course completed three times in Asahikawa, Hokkaido. The course winds through Hokkaido's snow-covered forests and open terrain. Named after the Swedish Vasaloppet, the Japanese edition adapts the long-distance skiing tradition to Hokkaido's extraordinary snow conditions. The loop format provides spectator-friendly racing with multiple pass-throughs of the start/finish area.
course.surface: Machine-groomed skate course through Hokkaido terrain
course.grooming: Good standard, maintained with Japanese precision despite heavy snow
features: Named after the original Swedish Vasaloppet
Set in Asahikawa — one of Japan's coldest cities
Loop format allows multiple spectator viewing points

nordic_lab_rating.score_note: Maximum snow reliability — Hokkaido is one of the snowiest places on Earth. Strong community and organization scores reflecting Japanese event culture. Over 40 years of history gives solid prestige. The Vasaloppet name provides international recognition, though the course itself is relatively modest.

history.summary: Founded in 1981, Vasaloppet Japan brought the Scandinavian long-distance skiing tradition to Hokkaido. Named with permission from the original Swedish Vasaloppet, the race established itself in Asahikawa, one of Japan's coldest and snowiest cities. The event has grown over four decades to attract around 2,000 participants and serves as Japan's premier cross-country ski marathon. Hokkaido's extraordinary snow reliability — fed by Siberian air masses crossing the Sea of Japan — makes it one of the most snow-sure races in the world.
notable_facts: Named after the Swedish Vasaloppet with official blessing
Founded in 1981 — over 40 years of history
Asahikawa regularly records Japan's coldest temperatures
Hokkaido receives some of the heaviest snowfall on Earth
Japan's premier cross-country ski marathon event
15km loop format inspired by Japanese running culture
